attributes.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a 3-year history of fatigue and arthralgia. He has a medical history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, for which he takes metformin. He consumes 2 beers/day on average. On examination he has an irregularly irregular pulse and mild tenderness on palpation of the right upper quadrant of his abdomen. He also has significant hyperpigmentation of the skin, most apparent on his face which looks very brown. He reports his mother developed a similar colouring to her skin later in life.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the key initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
The patient presents with symptoms suggestive of hereditary haemochromatosis, a common autosomal recessive disorder that usually presents in the fifth decade of life in men and sixth decade in women.

The initial investigation in this case should be iron studies. If there is iron overload with raised ferritin level and transferrin saturation, a HFE gene studies should be performed. The normal reference ranges for ferritin is <200mcg/L for women and <300mcg/L for men; normal transferrin saturation is <40% in women and <50% in men.

An HFE gene mutation leads to low levels of hepcidin which result in increased intestinal iron absorption. Over many years, iron accumulates in a variety of organs contributing to organ dysfunction: pituitary (hypogonadism), liver (cirrhosis), pancreas (diabetes), heart (dilated cardiomyopathy and cardiac arrhythmias) and skin (hyperpigmentation due to iron and melanin deposition). Other symptoms include fatigue, arthralgia most commonly affecting the 2nd and 3rd metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints.

Additionally, these patients are at an increased risk of infection from Yersinia enterocolitica and listeria. If a patient develops cirrhosis, they also have a 20-fold risk of hepatocellular carcinoma. Due to this increased risk, they require regular surveillance with abdominal ultrasound and AFP levels.

HFE gene testing will not 'confirm' the diagnosis as patients who have the C282Y mutation may not have iron overload. Whilst it is a necessary test to perform, a positive gene test alone will not determine the cause of the patient's symptoms.

Liver function tests may be elevated in a patient with iron overload. However, there will likely be mild derangement and it does not help determine the cause of the patient's symptoms.

A Synacthen test would be performed if there was suspicion of Addison's disease. Whilst some patient's may present with fatigue, they typically also report anorexia, generalised weakness and weight loss. Hypotension may be evident. Addison's disease can cause 'bronzing' of the skin, though this is different to the hyperpigmentation seen in iron overload, and it typically affects sun exposed areas and pressure points.

Serum aldosterone / renin ratio is a test used to diagnose primary hyperaldosteronism. Typically, patients with this condition present with hypertension, hypokalaemia and headaches.

Venesection is the first line treatment for iron overload in primary haemochromatosis and is performed once or twice a week until a satisfactory result. The goal for treatment is attaining a ferritin level <50mcg/l.
learningPoints: Iron studies should be the initial test for patients with suspected iron overload
references: Brissot P, Pietrangelo A, Adams PC, et al. Haemochromatosis. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2018 Apr 5;4:18016.  doi: 10.1038/nrdp.2018.16.
Porter JL, Rawla P. Hemochromatosis [Internet]. Nih.gov. StatPearls Publishing; 2019. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430862/
